sentences.text: It's Janet Applefield, J-A-N-E-T A-P-P-L-E-F-I-E-L-D.
Gustawa Singer, G-U-S-T-A-W-A, Singer, S-I-N-G-E-R.
My nickname was Dzidzia, D-Z-I-D-Z-I-A.
Just it means baby.
And I had other names during the war.
My name was Krystina Antoszkiewicz, K-R-Y-S-T-I-N-A A-N-T-O-S-Z-K-I-E-W-I-C-Z.
No, I think that's all.
June 4th, 1935.
I was born in Krakow, Poland, C-R-A-C-OW, Poland, P-O-L-A-N-D.
Well, I lived in a little town, Nowy Targ, outside of Krakow, with a large extended family.
I had a baby sister.
And I was the first grandchild in the family.
And my father was one of six brothers.
And my mother was one of three sisters.
And one set of grandparents, the Singers, lived in Nowy Targ.
My other grandparents lived in Wadowice, a little village-- same place where the current Pope is from.
And I remember a lot of fun things.
I remember my uncles doing a lot of fun things with me in particular.
My uncle Arthur used to take me out on his motorcycle, and we would go to the candy store, and he would buy me things.
I remember preparation for the holidays when my mother and my grandmother used to cook and bake.
I remember making challah, braiding challah with them, and making noodles.
Everything was made from scratch, so it took a long time to prepare.
And I remember the house being cleaned and-- but in particular, I recall the gypsy woman used to come.
And she used to scrub-- you know, scrub the floors and get the whole house ready.
For the holi-- like for Friday night, for every Friday night when, you know, we'd have our Shabbos supper.
We had chicken soup, of course-- chicken, fish, cold fish.
I think it was carp.
And it was cooked with raisins.
And I remember the gelatin from the fish.
I remember certain dishes that to this day, I kind of favor, you know, like sliced cumbers called mizeria with cream and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] borscht.
You know, I mean, different things, but not always the same things.
No.
No, we lived in a-- I think it was an apartment.
I don't remember exactly where we lived, but I-- it wasn't a house.
But I remember a couple incidents that happened.
Once-- well, I must have been two, three years old.
There was a beautiful table cloth on the table, in the dining room table.
My mother did a craft called tatting.
It was kind of an embroidery, but lace-- sort of lace making.
And I remember having a wonderful time cutting it up with scissors.
And another time, I remember the housekeeper was cleaning out the bird cage.
And the bird was on the balcony.
And she told me to hold the door closed, you know, not to let him out.
Of course, I did let him out and the neighbors running around looking for a canary that was flying around.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Different little things-- I remember the first time my parents went out.
I was still in a crib.
The crib was made out of like, a netting, like a rope, rope netting.
And my parents were going out for the evening and leaving me with the housekeeper.
And I remember you know, carrying on, screaming.
And that was my first experience with separation.
And it's so incredible, because I remember the design of the nightgown that I was wearing.
And I must have been really little, because I was still in a crib.
So things like that, you know, those very early memories-- but mainly the family and the warmth of the family.
Her name was Amalja Nabel-- her maiden name.
It was an arranged meeting.
And the story goes that when my mother first saw my father, she locked herself in a room and would not come out, because my father was a red head.
In those days, red heads were really stigmatized.
And you know, she didn't want to come out.
But I guess-- I don't know how or how she was convinced, but she did come out.
[LAUGHS] But so it was an arranged meeting.
She got married in 1934.
And she was born in 1910.
So it would make her 24.
No, she was from Wadowice.
That was a village-- not far.
You know, we used to go there by train.
So it wasn't very far.
That's right.
Lived in Nowy Targ.
My grandfather Nabel was very religious.
And my other grandparents-- I think they practiced it, but I don't think they were-- I don't think they were very-- I think my father sort of put up a fuss, even though he did it.
Because you know, there was either orthodoxy or there was nothing.
So-- but I remember the men praying, you know, with the teffilin.
But as I said, my grandfather, Jakob, was very religious.
And we used to go there very often.
I remember one time I was there, and I got very sick.
I had scarlet fever.
And so the treatment in those days is to be kept in a darkened room, and you had to-- you couldn't eat.
You had to be on a strict diet.
And I remember everybody would come in the room.
And I would want to smell their breath to see what they ate.
And to this day, I still smell tomato soup.
And I love tomato soup.
[LAUGHTER]
There were cousins.
There was-- my father's brother was married.
And they had a little girl, [?
Anette, ?] Anushka.
And then my mother's sister was married, and they had a little boy, [?
Marzelic, ?] so I had little cousins.
I just remember the balcony, where the bird-- I let the bird out.
And I remember the dining room.
And I remember my crib.
And I remember one time stealing [LAUGHS] a little hot dog.
They were all linked together, and I just pulled them and ate them raw-- things like that.
But I don't remember the layout of the whole apartment.
I think I remembered more because I've been there in recent years, and so I pictured that, but-- and also from the photographs that I have.
Actually, the village has not changed at all.
It's basically how it was, with the exception of the main square, which is called the [POLISH].
That used to be all cobblestone, and now it's a big parking lot, you know, that they use for parking.
Oh, also I remember the market day.
I think it was on a Thursday.
And I particularly remember that, because that was the day when all the peasants-- the peasants were called [POLISH].
And they were-- came from the mountains.
And they came to shop that day.
And the market was right in the [POLISH].
Today it's not there anymore.
It's a different location.
But-- and that was a very good business day for my grandfather, who had the houseware, home improvement type of store.
In Nowy Targ, right in the center of the town, in the [POLISH].
Well, my father worked with my grandfather-- not willingly.
He-- he had-- he wanted to become a dentist.
And actually, he was-- the story goes that my father was on the train going to-- enrolled in a technical school to learn dentistry, and my grandfather pulled him off the train and made him stay.
And being the oldest son, he had to do that.
So my-- the other brothers sort of got away from-- [LAUGHS] wherever--
Well, there was six altogether, including my father-- six brothers.
No, she did not, no.
There was.
And I understand that pre-war, it was about 2,500.
And there was a synagogue.
And--
No, I do not.
Not really.
I remember the-- the nurse maid that took care of me.
Her name was [?
Osza ?].
I remember her.
She lived in the house, yes.
I had a baby sister.
Her name was Sarenka.
Well, it was Sarah, but we called her Sarenka.
I was older.
Yes, I was born in 1935.
And she was born, I think, '38 or '39.
Well, my sister died in 1940 or '41, after the war had began.
She was sick with diphtheria.
There were no medications.
There was no-- there was no medical treatment at all.
And she died.
No, I don't remember any changes before the war.
After the war began, I became much more aware of something happen-- being very, very wrong.
I remember some of my toys.
I rem-- I don't remember playing with-- I actually don't remember playing with other children.
But I remember the toys.
And I remember the family.
And I remember going out with my mother for walks.
I remember the winters.
I remember riding in a horse-drawn sled in the wintertime.
That's how we really got around-- or even on skis.
My father used to say he would keep a pair of skis at the door, so if he wanted to get around, he would use-- because there was so much snow all the time.
The family vacations were mainly to visit my grandparents.
And--
It seemed like it was a lot.
I don't know.
I used to like to go there, because they had a big garden.
They had a big house, and there was a garden behind their house.
My grandfather used to make wine.
And my grandmother used to can the fruits.
She had three sisters.
She was one of three.
Right.
She was the-- she was the middle daughter.
They lived-- one aunt lived with my grandparents.
She was married, my oldest-- the oldest daughter.
And the youngest daughter had gotten married, I guess after my mother and father got married.
And they lived in a town called Katowice.
Also--
Also a train ride away, short train ride, all within maybe half hour or an hour, or something like that.
Krakow was the large city nearby.
I don't remember going there, but I think we did.
Because we had family there.
My mother had family.
We would take the train.
I unfortunately remember so many other details, but I don't remember my mother very well.
I just know some of the things that I've been told about her.
I've been told that she was very bright, and she had a very good business sense-- something that I don't have.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] but otherwise, I don't remember her.
So some of the things I'm going to tell you is what I heard my father tell me, because I don't remember-- actually remember.
But he said that he was awakened at 5 o'clock in the morning hearing shots, and shooting, or explosions, or-- and he turned on the radio.
And he heard that Germany had invaded Poland.
At that point, my father took me, and my mother, and my sister to my other grandparents' house.
I'm not sure exactly at what point, whether he waited days, or probably you know, the next day or whatever.
But he returned to Nowy Targ.
And so I stayed with my grandparents, my mother, and my sister.
And at that point, my grandfather loaded up the wagon-- horse and wagon.
My grandfather was in the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he traded hay and everything to do with horses.
He had that kind of a shop.
So he loaded up the wagon with the family-- my aunt and uncle-- two aunts and uncles, my mother and myself, my sister-- and as many belongings as we could fit into this wagon and two horses.
And we joined a caravan of people heading eastward.
And there were people walking, riding on horses or any which-- bicycles.
And we were traveling for days and days and days, heading toward Russia.
During this travel, I do remember a-- an airplane, a German plane.
I think it was German.
It may not have been a German, but an airplane that was shot down and fell out of the sky.
We were being shot at.
There were bullets.
And we-- often, we had to get out of the wagon and hide in ditches and wait till the shooting stopped.
Sometimes the roads were blocked.
Sometimes bridges were out and we couldn't get across.
And so we would have to go across the river.
And so this continued until-- until we reached Russia.
Well, my father returned to Nowy Targ.
And he and his brothers and father joined the Polish army, if there was such a thing, and tried to repel the Germans.
Apparently, the Polish army disbanded very shortly and they just came back.
At that point, my father and his brothers [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] joined this caravan of people heading eastward.
My grandmother and my grandfather and one uncle remained.
They chose not to go.
So my father finally arrived in Russia, but he could-- he didn't know where we were.
We were separated.
He didn't know where we were.
My father said-- so my father was in Lvov.
We were in a town called [?
Wutsk. ?] I don't think that was terribly far from-- the two towns were close by.
And my father advertised in the newspaper looking for us.
He mentioned me-- he particularly mentioned that I had a birthmark on my leg, and so he mentioned that.
And in time, he did find us and joined us.
Of course, at that point, my father and my grandfather and all the men had to think of a way of earning a living.
And they decided to take dairy products from the country, bring it to the city, and that's how they earned some money.
We all lived together in one big-- it wasn't so big, but one ar-- one house.
And after a while, my father and my mother and I and my sister left them, because we were just too crowded.
And we went to a town called Winniki.
At that point, my father got a job working in a coke-- like a peat factory, you know, for coal.
And so that's what he was doing.
And I-- I remember my mother got very sick one time.
And once again, there was very little medical treatment.
She-- I remember this, because it was so frightening.
She had leeches put behind her ear and they sucked out the blood.
I guess she must have had infection or fever.
And she got better.
Winniki.
Mhm, right.
No, I don't recall being in contact with them.
I don't remember-- I don't remember that at all.
I recall that there was something terribly wrong.
No one sat me down and explained anything.
But somehow, I knew-- I just-- I always felt, as a child during the war, I knew what was going on, without having really been told.
Because I heard the adults talking.
But I knew there was something bad happening.
Months, maybe-- months.
And my-- that's when my sister got-- my baby sister got sick with diphtheria and she died.
I remember her being sick.
I don't remember the burial or the death, or I don't remember that.
I think that must have been in 1940, or close to '41-- something like that. '40, '41.
